skill.name: data-visualization
skill.description: Guides DBX Studio users in choosing chart types, writing read-oriented SQL queries, and configuring generated charts.
skill.summary: Create DBX Studio charts from SQL-backed data with clear chart selection guidance.
skill.author: Dbxstudio

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown backticks, SQL examples, and plain text guidance as external commands, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance. Manual review found no executable code, no network access, no prompt injection, and no evidence of malicious intent. The remaining concern is normal operational risk from generating read-oriented SQL for charts.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misidentified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static external command findings are false positives. Lines 10, 14-19, 24-25, 30, 39, 43-47, 52, 58, 61, 67, and 70 use Markdown code formatting or fenced SQL and JSON examples, not Ruby or shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are Markdown prose, tables, or fenced examples. No command interpreter, Ruby backtick execution, or executable script is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak cryptography findings at lines 3, 36, 56, 76, and 77 do not reference cryptographic algorithms. They occur in a chart description, SQL aggregation examples, and design guidance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic algorithm usage. The lines are unrelated to cryptography.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Finding Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The static system reconnaissance finding at line 79 is guidance to aggregate large data sets before charting. It does not inspect the host, enumerate files, read environment details, or collect system metadata.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a data visualization best practice about aggregation. There is no system command, filesystem access, or reconnaissance behavior.

low_findings.title: Generated SQL Requires Data Access Controls
low_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to write SQL queries for visualization. This is legitimate for a data skill, but deployments should rely on normal database permissions, review of sensitive columns, and read-only access where possible.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file clearly asks the assistant to create data queries, but the examples are read-only aggregate queries. Risk depends on the connected database permissions.

content.value_statement: Raw database results can be hard to compare and explain. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code choose chart types, write SQL-backed queries, and configure readable DBX Studio charts.
actual_capabilities: Selects chart types for comparisons, trends, proportions, distributions, and correlations.
Defines chart configuration fields such as title, axes, chart type, and grouping.
Provides SQL query patterns for top categories, time series, and status breakdowns.
Encourages aggregation before charting large result sets.
Guides readable titles and axis labels for DBX Studio charts.
limitations: Does not include executable chart rendering code in the skill file.
Assumes a separate generate_chart tool is available in DBX Studio.
SQL examples are generic and may need changes for each database schema.
Does not define database permission checks or sensitive data policies.

use_cases.title: Build revenue dashboards
use_cases.description: Create bar and line charts that summarize revenue by category, date, or product group.

use_cases.title: Explore operational status
use_cases.description: Turn order, ticket, or workflow statuses into simple proportion and count charts.

use_cases.title: Compare product performance
use_cases.description: Use aggregate SQL and chart guidance to compare products, regions, or customer segments.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic chart
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a simple visualization.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a bar chart that compares total orders by status. Use clear labels and limit the result to the most important categories.

prompt_templates.title: Show a trend over time
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for a time series chart.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a line chart showing daily revenue for the last 30 days. Aggregate by day and include a descriptive chart title.

prompt_templates.title: Compare ranked categories
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for ranked business performance.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a bar chart of the top 10 product categories by revenue. Sort highest to lowest and use human-readable axis names.

prompt_templates.title: Choose the best visualization
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request where the chart type is not known.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the available fields for orders and recommend the best chart for finding relationships, trends, or distributions. Then create the chart configuration.

output_examples.output: A bar chart titled Monthly Revenue by Product Category, with category on the horizontal axis and total revenue on the vertical axis.

output_examples.input: I need to see order trends for the last month.
output_examples.output: A line chart with one point per day, sorted by date, showing total revenue or order count over the last 30 days.

output_examples.input: What share of orders are in each status?
output_examples.output: A pie chart for order status counts, using no more than seven slices and grouping small values as Other when needed.

best_practices: Use aggregate queries before charting large data sets.
Choose chart types based on the question, not on personal preference.
Use descriptive titles and human-readable axis labels.
anti_patterns: Do not use pie charts for more than seven categories.
Do not chart raw row-level data when aggregation answers the question.
Do not use vague titles or raw database column names as labels.

faq.question: What chart types does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers bar, line, pie, scatter, area, and histogram charts.

faq.question: Does this skill render charts by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance for configuring charts through a separate generate_chart tool.

faq.question: Can it write SQL queries for charts?
faq.answer: Yes. It gives read-oriented SQL patterns for common visualization needs.

faq.question: Is it suitable for large data sets?
faq.answer: Yes, when queries aggregate results before charting and avoid raw row-level output.

faq.question: Does it include database security controls?
faq.answer: No. Database permissions and sensitive data controls must be enforced by the host environment.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this marketplace entry?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
